title: L03.2.sets dicts
body:
Toolkit for Finance and Financial Decision Making Lecture 3.2: Sets and dictionaries

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin UNSW

Overview

Sets:  Properties and construction  Commonly used methods and expressions Dictionaries  Properties and construction  Commonly used methods and expressions

Data Structures: Sets  Data Structures: Sets

A set is an unordered collection of unique Python objects.

Sets have the following key properties: Unordered:  Sets do not have a fixed order that can be relied on across different runs, Python versions, or operating systems. Mutable:  Objects may be added to or removed from the list. Uniqueness:  An object can only appear once in the set.

Use Sets instead of Lists when you want to ensure there is only one reference to the object in the collection and order does not matter.

Data Structures: Sets - Construction

Sets can be constructed using literals of the form: { expression1, expression2, ...}

An opening curly brace { A comma-separated list of one or more expressions A closed curly brace } Python ensures that the set contains only one of each element. 1 # A set of the first three integers 2 {1, 2, 3} 3 # {1, 2, 3} 5 # A set of the first three integers 6 {1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 3, 2, 1} 7 # {1, 2, 3}

Data Structures: Sets - Construction

Sets can also be constructed using the set constructor: set(iterable)

1 # Construct a set from a list 2 set([1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 3, 2, 1]) 3 # {1, 2, 3} 5 # Construct and empty set 6 set() 7 # set()

Important: The literal {} will not construct an empty set. To construct an empty set, you must use the set constructor

Data Structures: Sets - Construction

Sets may only include hashable objects.

An object is hashable if it has a fixed, unchanging hash value. Hash values are integers that summarise the object’s contents. Python uses hash values to quickly look up objects in data structures like sets. Hash values are distinct from memory locations. A memory location tells you where an object is; a hash value tells you something about what an object is. Typically, only immutable objects are hashable.

Example: Lists cannot be elements of a set because they are mutable and cannot be hashed

Data Structures: Sets - Indexing

As an unordered group of Python objects, Sets do not support indexing.

Data Structures: Sets - Slicing

As an unordered group of Python objects, Sets do not support slicing.

Data Structures: Sets - Commonly used methods

Sets are instances of the set class, which provides many useful methods

Like list methods, many set methods will modify the set in place. my_set.add(x) -> None Add element x to the set if it is not already present (Modifies my_set returning None) 1 s = set() 2 s.add(1) # s = {1} 3 s.add(2) # s = {1, 2} 4 s.add(2) # s = {1, 2}

my_set.remove(x) -> None Remove element x from the set, throwing a KeyError if x is not present (Modifies my_set returning None) 1 s = {'a', 'b'} 2 s.remove('a') # s = {'b'} 3 s.remove('c') # KeyError: 'c'

You will encounter many other set methods as we progress through the course.

Data Structures: Sets - Commonly used expressions

Some key set operations are performed using operators or built-in functions, not methods

x in my_set -> bool Check if an element is in a my_set (Not an instance method) 1 s = {1, 2, 3} 2 1 in s # -> True 3 4 in s # -> False

x not in my_set -> bool Check if an element is not in a my_set (Not an instance method) 1 s = {1, 2, 3} 2 1 not in s # -> False 3 4 not in s # -> True

len(my_set) -> int Get the number of elements in a my_set 1 s = {1, 2, 3} 2 len(s) # -> 3

Dictionaries are the most complicated data structure in the Python standard library.

Real dictionaries link words and definitions Python dictionaries link key objects to value objects.  Dictionaries serve as a hybrid between sets and lists.  Like sets, each key appears once.  Like lists, we can have a variety of object values.

Daily stock prices are a natural fit for a dictionary:

| Date | Price | | '2020-01-02' | 7.1600 | | '2020-01-03' | 7.1900 | | '2020-01-06' | 7.0000 | | '2020-01-07' | 7.1000 | | '2020-01-08' | 6.8600 | | '2020-01-09' | 6.9500 | | '2020-01-10' | 7.0000 | | '2020-01-13' | 7.0200 | | '2020-01-14' | 7.1100 | | '2020-01-15' | 7.0400 |

Daily stock prices are a natural fit for a dictionary:

| Date | Price | | '2020-01-02' | 7.1600 | | '2020-01-03' | 7.1900 | | '2020-01-06' | 7.0000 | | '2020-01-07' | 7.1000 | | '2020-01-08' | 6.8600 | | '2020-01-09' | 6.9500 | | '2020-01-10' | 7.0000 | | '2020-01-13' | 7.0200 | | '2020-01-14' | 7.1100 | | '2020-01-15' | 7.0400 |

Python dictionaries link key objects to value objects.

Assuming we want use look up stock prices by date: Date is the key Price is the value

A dictionary is an unordered collection of Python key-value pairs.

Dictionaries have the following key properties: Key-value pairs  Data is stored as pairs: Each key object is associated with a specific value object. Unordered:  The keys do not have a fixed order that can be relied on across different runs, Python versions, or operating systems.

Mutable:  Key-value pairs may be added to or removed from the dictionary.  The values associated with a key may be replaced.

Keys must be hashable objects. Values can be any Python object and do not need to be unique.

Data Structures: Dictionaries - Construction

Dictionaries are typically constructed using literals of the form: { key1: value1, key2: value2, ...}

An opening curly brace { Zero or more key-value pairs  Keys and values are linked by a colon  Key-value pair groups are separated by commas A closing curly brace } Python will ensure that the keys of the dictionary are unique, using the last value when a key is reused.

Data Structures: Dictionaries - Construction

1 # A stock price dictionary 2 prc_dic = { '2020-01-02': 7.16, 3 '2020-01-03': 7.19, 4 '2020-01-03': 8.00, 5 } 6 # {'2020-01-02': 7.16, '2020-01-03': 8.0} 8 # An empty dictionary using empty braces 9 empty_dict = {} 10 # An empty dictionary using the 11 d = dict()

A dictionary definition may span multiple lines, which improves readability. End a key-value line with a comma and put the next key-value pair on the line below.

Data Structures: Dictionaries - Queries

We look up or “query” each value by its key (or label):. Query syntax: dictionary[key] 1 prc_dic = { '2020-01-02': 7.1600, 2 '2020-01-03': 7.1900, 3 '2020-01-06': 7.0000, 4 '2020-01-07': 7.1000, 5 '2020-01-08': 6.8600, 6 } 7 # Get the value stored under '2020-01-02' 8 prc_dic['2020-01-02'] 9 # 7.16

Data Structures: Dictionaries - Queries

We look up or “query” each value by its key (or label):. Query syntax: dictionary[key] 1 prc_dic = { '2020-01-02': 7.1600, 2 '2020-01-03': 7.1900, 3 '2020-01-06': 7.0000, 4 '2020-01-07': 7.1000, 5 '2020-01-08': 6.8600, 6 } 7 # Get the value stored under '2020-01-02' 8 prc_dic['2020-01-02'] 9 # 7.16

Attempting to access the value under a key that is not in the dictionary gives a KeyError: 1 prc_dic['2020-13-13'] 2 # KeyError: '2020-13-13'

The query is one-way; we cannot look up the key that corresponds to a value 1 prc_dic[7.1600] 2 # KeyError: 7.16

Dictionaries handle adding key-value pairs and updating value pairs as one operation:

Adding: Add a new key-value pair to a dictionary Updating: Changing the value object for a key object that is already in the dictionary.

example_dict[key] = value Add or update a single key-value pair (Modifies example_dict. Not an instance method) 1 d = {"a": 1} 2 d["a"] = 2 # d = {'a': 2} 3 d["b"] = 3 # d = {'a': 2, 'b': 3}

example_dict.update(key: value, key: value, ...) -> None Add or update multiple key-value pairs (Modifies example_dict returning None) 1 d_update_with_dict = {'a': 1, 'b': 2} 2 d_update_with_dict.update({'a': 2, 'c': 3 }) 3 print(f"After update d_update_with_dict is {d_update_with_dict}") 5 d_update_with_list = {'a': 1, 'b': 2 } 6 d_update_with_list.update([('a', 3), ('c', 5 )]) 7 print(f"After update d_update_with_list is {d_update_with_list}")

example_dict.pop(key) -> object Remove a key and its associated value (Modifies example_dict returning value object associated with key) 1 d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2 } 2 ret = d.pop('a') 3 print(f"After popping 'a', d is now {d}. The value {ret} was returned.") 4 # Output: After popping 'a', d is now {'b': 2}. The value 1 was returned. 6 d.pop('c') 7 # KeyError: 'c'

Some key dicionary operations are performed using operators or built-in functions, not methods

x in example_dict -> bool Check if an element is a key in example_dict (Not an instance method) 2 'a' in d # -> True 3 'z' in d # -> False

Some key dicionary operations are performed using operators or built-in functions, not methods

x in example_dict -> bool Check if an element is a key in example_dict (Not an instance method) 2 'a' in d # -> True 3 'z' in d # -> False

x not in example_dict -> bool Check if an element is a key not in example_dict (Not an instance method) 2 'a' not in d # -> False 3 'z' not in d # -> True

Some key dicionary operations are performed using operators or built-in functions, not methods

x in example_dict -> bool Check if an element is a key in example_dict (Not an instance method) 2 'a' in d # -> True 3 'z' in d # -> False

x not in example_dict -> bool Check if an element is a key not in example_dict (Not an instance method) 2 'a' not in d # -> False 3 'z' not in d # -> True

len(example_dict) -> int Get the number of key-value pairs in example_dict 2 len(d) # -> 3

Data Structures: Sets and Dictionaries – Key Takeaways

Sets provide a simple and efficient way to manage unique elements.

Ensure uniqueness automatically (no duplicates) Allow fast membership checks (in, not in) Work only with hashable elements

Data Structures: Sets and Dictionaries – Key Takeaways

Sets provide a simple and efficient way to manage unique elements.

Ensure uniqueness automatically (no duplicates) Allow fast membership checks (in, not in) Work only with hashable elements Dictionaries store data as key–value pairs, offering fast lookups by key.

Keys must be hashable Values can be any Python object and do not need to be unique Keys are unique; assigning a new value to an existing key overwrites the old one Provide efficient queries, insertions, and updates by key
